title: Structured Data Extraction Pipeline
description:
You are the data engineering architect at MedReview, a healthcare analytics company that processes clinical trial reports, FDA submissions, and peer-reviewed medical research papers. MedReview's core product ingests unstructured medical documents (PDFs, HTML, scanned images) and extracts structured data: drug names, dosages, patient demographics, adverse events, efficacy endpoints, and statistical results. This structured data feeds into a proprietary database used by pharmaceutical companies for competitive intelligence and regulatory planning.

The current extraction pipeline uses a combination of regex patterns and custom NLP models, achieving approximately 78% accuracy on drug-dosage extraction and 65% accuracy on adverse event classification. Leadership wants to rebuild the extraction layer using Claude to improve accuracy to 95%+ while reducing the 6-month development cycle for adding new extraction targets to under 2 weeks.

The architect must design a structured extraction system using Zod schemas for output validation. Each document type has a different extraction schema: Clinical Trial Reports require study ID, phase (I/II/III/IV), drug name, comparator, primary endpoint, secondary endpoints, patient count, demographics breakdown, efficacy results with confidence intervals, and adverse events with severity grades. FDA Submission Letters require application number, drug name, indication, decision (approved/complete response/refuse to file), conditions, and post-marketing requirements. Research Papers require title, authors, journal, publication date, study design, sample size, key findings, limitations, and funding sources.

A critical challenge is handling optional and nullable fields. Medical documents are inconsistent — some clinical trial reports include demographic breakdowns by race and ethnicity, while others report only age and sex. Some adverse events have severity grades (Grade 1–5), while others use descriptive terms (mild/moderate/severe). The Zod schemas must handle this variability gracefully: required fields that are always present (drug name, study phase), optional fields that may or may not appear (demographic subgroups), and nullable fields where the information exists but is explicitly stated as 'not reported' or 'not applicable.'

The validation-retry pattern is essential for maintaining extraction quality. When Claude's initial extraction attempt fails Zod validation — for example, returning a string '200 patients' instead of a number 200 for patient_count, or providing an adverse event severity as 'moderate' instead of the expected enum value — the system must feed the validation errors back to Claude and request a corrected extraction. This retry loop must be bounded (maximum 3 attempts) and must track which specific fields failed validation so that subsequent attempts can focus on correcting those fields.

MedReview processes approximately 2,000 documents per week, making the Anthropic Batch API essential for cost efficiency. Batch processing allows MedReview to submit documents in bulk at a 50% discount compared to real-time API calls. However, batch processing introduces new challenges: how to handle partial batch failures (some documents succeed, others fail), how to match batch results back to their source documents, how to prioritize urgent documents (e.g., newly published Phase III results) for real-time processing while routing routine documents to batch.

The system must also handle extraction conflicts within a single document. A clinical trial report might state '450 patients enrolled' in the abstract but '423 patients completed the study' in the results section. The extraction schema must capture both values with their provenance (which section of the document each came from) so downstream consumers can make informed decisions about which value to use.

Quality requirements: 95% accuracy on required fields, 90% accuracy on optional fields, less than 1% false positive rate for adverse event extraction (critical for patient safety), and complete provenance tracking for every extracted value. Processing throughput: 2,000 documents per week via batch, with real-time processing capability for up to 50 urgent documents per day.

questions.scenario: You are defining the Zod schema for extracting clinical trial data from research papers. The schema must include: study_id (always present), phase (enum: 'I', 'II', 'III', 'IV'), drug_name (always present), patient_count (integer), demographics (an object with age_mean, age_sd, percent_female — but not all papers report all demographics), and adverse_events (an array of objects with event_name, severity, and count). A junior engineer has defined all fields as required z.string() types.
questions.question: What is wrong with defining all extraction fields as required z.string() types, and what schema design is correct?

options.A: Nothing is wrong — strings can represent any data type, and downstream systems can parse them
options.B: The schema must use precise types for each field: z.string() for study_id and drug_name, z.enum() for phase, z.number().int().positive() for patient_count, z.object().partial() for demographics (allowing missing subfields), and z.array() of z.object() for adverse_events — with .optional() on fields that may not be present in every document and .nullable() for fields explicitly marked as 'not reported'
options.C: All fields should be z.any() to handle the variability in medical documents
options.D: Use z.union() for every field to accept either string or number formats

questions.explanation: Precise Zod types serve two critical purposes: (1) they guide Claude toward producing correctly typed output — when the schema specifies z.number().int().positive() for patient_count, Claude returns 200 instead of '200 patients'; (2) they catch extraction errors at validation time rather than letting malformed data enter the database. Key design patterns: z.enum() for constrained values (phase must be exactly I/II/III/IV); z.object().partial() for objects where some subfields may be absent (demographics without ethnicity data); .optional() for fields that some documents lack entirely; .nullable() for fields explicitly noted as unavailable. Option A loses type safety — '200 patients' in a numeric field requires downstream parsing. Option C provides no validation at all. Option D adds unnecessary complexity when most fields have clear types.
questions.examTrap: Option D (z.union for everything) seems flexible but defeats the purpose of schema validation. If every field accepts string or number, validation catches almost nothing. The exam rewards understanding that strict schemas IMPROVE extraction quality by guiding Claude's output format.

questions.scenario: MedReview's extraction pipeline validates Claude's output against the Zod schema. When validation fails — for example, Claude returns 'approximately 450' for patient_count (expected z.number().int()) or 'moderate' for adverse_event_severity (expected z.enum(['Grade 1', 'Grade 2', 'Grade 3', 'Grade 4', 'Grade 5'])) — the system needs to automatically retry with corrective feedback. The current implementation simply retries with the same prompt, which produces the same errors.
questions.question: How should the validation-retry pattern be implemented to maximize first-retry success?

options.A: Retry with a higher temperature to get different output
options.B: Feed the specific Zod validation errors back to Claude in the retry prompt — including which fields failed, what was expected vs received, and the correct format — then ask Claude to return only the corrected fields merged with the previously successful extractions, with a maximum of 3 retry attempts before falling back to human review
options.C: Apply regex transformations to fix common errors (strip 'approximately', map 'moderate' to 'Grade 3') before validation
options.D: Relax the Zod schema to accept the variations Claude produces

questions.explanation: The validation-retry pattern works by creating a feedback loop: (1) validate Claude's output against the Zod schema; (2) collect specific validation failures — not generic errors, but 'patient_count: expected integer, received string "approximately 450"' and 'adverse_event_severity: expected one of [Grade 1-5], received "moderate"'; (3) construct a retry prompt that includes the original document, the successful extractions (no need to re-extract these), and the specific failures with corrective guidance; (4) ask Claude to return corrected values only, reducing token cost; (5) merge corrected values with previously successful ones; (6) re-validate the merged result. This approach maximizes first-retry success because Claude knows exactly what went wrong and what format is expected. Bounded to 3 attempts to prevent infinite loops, with human review as fallback. Option A changes randomness but not understanding. Option C requires maintaining a growing list of heuristics. Option D degrades data quality.
questions.examTrap: Option C (regex post-processing) seems practical but creates a maintenance nightmare. Every new edge case requires a new regex rule, and rules can conflict. The validation-retry pattern is self-correcting — Claude learns from the error feedback and handles new variations automatically.

questions.scenario: MedReview processes 2,000 documents per week. Using real-time API calls costs $0.08 per document on average. The Anthropic Batch API offers a 50% discount for asynchronous processing. The team wants to route most documents through batch processing but needs real-time processing for urgent documents (newly published Phase III results, FDA decisions). They need a system that handles batch submission, result retrieval, partial failure recovery, and priority routing.
questions.question: How should MedReview architect its document processing to optimally use the Batch API?

options.A: Process all documents in real-time and ignore the Batch API to keep the architecture simple
options.B: Implement a dual-track processing system: a priority classifier routes urgent documents (Phase III results, FDA decisions) to real-time processing, while routine documents are queued and submitted to the Batch API in daily batches — with each batch item including a correlation ID that maps results back to source documents, a retry queue for failed batch items, and monitoring that tracks batch completion rates and per-document costs
options.C: Submit all documents to the Batch API and poll for results every hour
options.D: Process documents in micro-batches of 10 every 15 minutes as a compromise

questions.explanation: A dual-track system optimizes cost while maintaining responsiveness for urgent items: (1) Priority classification identifies time-sensitive documents (new Phase III data, FDA actions) that justify real-time processing costs; (2) Routine documents (historical papers, routine trial updates) are queued and submitted to the Batch API in bulk, saving 50% per document; (3) Correlation IDs are critical — each batch item needs a unique ID linking it to the source document, enabling result matching when the batch completes asynchronously; (4) Partial failure handling — when some batch items fail, only failed items are retried (via batch or real-time fallback), not the entire batch; (5) Cost monitoring tracks per-document and per-batch costs against budget. Option A wastes $3,200/month on unnecessary real-time processing. Option C adds unacceptable latency for all documents including urgent ones. Option D complicates batching without achieving significant cost savings.
questions.examTrap: Option C (batch everything) seems cost-optimal but fails the urgency requirement. A newly published Phase III result that shows unexpected safety signals cannot wait for a daily batch — it needs extraction within minutes, not hours. The exam tests understanding that batch processing is a cost optimization tool, not a universal solution.

questions.scenario: The extraction pipeline calls a DocumentParser tool that converts PDFs to text before passing content to Claude for extraction. This tool can fail in several ways: corrupted PDF (permanent — the file is unreadable), OCR timeout on large scanned documents (retryable — may succeed with a smaller page range), password-protected PDF (permanent unless password is provided), and rate limiting from the OCR service (retryable — will succeed after backoff). The current error handling catches all exceptions with a generic try/catch and returns 'extraction failed' for every error type.
questions.question: How should the extraction pipeline's error handling be structured to maximize document processing throughput?

options.A: Log all errors and skip failed documents, processing them manually later
options.B: Implement a classified error handling system: categorize each error as retryable or permanent with specific recovery strategies — OCR timeout triggers retry with reduced page range, rate limiting triggers exponential backoff, corrupted PDF is marked as permanent failure with notification to the data team, password-protected PDF is queued for credential lookup — and return structured error responses to the orchestrator with error_type, is_retryable, recovery_strategy, and partial_results if available
options.C: Retry every failed document 5 times with the same parameters before giving up
options.D: Pre-validate all documents before processing to prevent runtime errors

questions.explanation: Classified error handling maximizes throughput by applying the optimal recovery strategy for each error type: (1) OCR timeout — retry with a smaller page range (pages 1–50, then 51–100) to process the document in chunks, combining partial results; (2) Rate limiting — exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s) until the rate limit resets; (3) Corrupted PDF — immediate permanent failure with notification, no retry; (4) Password-protected — queue for credential lookup, then retry. Returning structured error responses enables the orchestrator to make informed decisions about retry scheduling and resource allocation. Partial results are preserved — if pages 1–50 extracted successfully before the timeout, those results are kept. Option A wastes the pipeline's ability to self-recover. Option C wastes resources retrying permanent failures and doesn't adapt retry strategy. Option D can't detect all runtime errors in advance.
questions.examTrap: Option C (uniform retry) is the most wasteful approach — retrying a corrupted PDF 5 times will always fail, consuming resources and delaying processing of other documents. Error classification is the key insight: different errors require different strategies.

questions.scenario: MedReview needs to extract adverse event data from clinical trial reports with less than 1% false positive rate. Individual Claude extraction attempts achieve 92% accuracy, but the team cannot tolerate false positives in adverse event reporting — a falsely reported adverse event could impact drug approval decisions. The architect is considering a multi-instance approach: running extraction multiple times and comparing results.
questions.question: What multi-instance review strategy maximizes adverse event extraction accuracy while controlling costs?

options.A: Run extraction once and manually review every adverse event — human review is the only way to achieve <1% false positive rate
options.B: Run extraction 3 times independently (with varied prompts emphasizing different aspects) and apply a consensus algorithm: adverse events reported by all 3 instances are confirmed (high confidence), events reported by 2 of 3 instances are flagged for human review, and events reported by only 1 instance are classified as potential false positives requiring verification — with the total cost being 3× single extraction but achieving the target false positive rate through statistical agreement
options.C: Run extraction 10 times and use majority voting to determine the final result
options.D: Use a single extraction with a very detailed prompt that lists every possible adverse event

questions.explanation: Multi-instance extraction with consensus provides statistical confidence while controlling costs. Three independent extraction instances, each with a slightly different prompt focus (one emphasizing severity grading, one emphasizing frequency counts, one emphasizing causal attribution), produce overlapping but independently derived results. The consensus algorithm: (1) 3/3 agreement = high confidence, auto-confirmed; (2) 2/3 agreement = medium confidence, routed to human review for the specific disagreed-upon fields; (3) 1/3 = low confidence, treated as potential false positive requiring verification. This approach leverages the law of independent errors — the probability of all 3 instances producing the same false positive is (0.08)³ = 0.05%, well below the 1% target. Cost is 3× but concentrated on the highest-value extraction target. Option A doesn't scale to 2,000 documents/week. Option C is unnecessarily expensive — 3 instances provide sufficient statistical power. Option D can't achieve the target rate from a single pass.
questions.examTrap: Option C (10 instances with majority voting) provides higher statistical confidence but at 10× the cost — and the marginal accuracy improvement from 3 to 10 instances is minimal. The exam rewards understanding of diminishing returns: 3 instances is the sweet spot for cost vs accuracy.

walkthrough:
Step 1: Design Extraction Schemas — Define Zod schemas for each document type: ClinicalTrialSchema, FDASubmissionSchema, ResearchPaperSchema. Use precise types (z.number().int() for counts, z.enum() for constrained values), .optional() for fields that may be absent, .nullable() for explicitly unreported values, and .partial() for sub-objects with variable completeness. Add .describe() annotations to guide Claude's extraction. Reference: src/content/questions/schema.ts demonstrates Zod schema patterns.

Step 2: Implement the Validation-Retry Loop — Build the feedback loop: extract → validate → if fails, construct retry prompt with specific failures → re-extract corrected fields → merge with successful extractions → re-validate. Bound to 3 attempts. Track per-field retry counts to identify systematically problematic fields. Fallback to human review after exhausting retries. Reference: src/tools/error-handling.ts shows error handling patterns.

Step 3: Build the Dual-Track Processing System — Implement priority classification that routes urgent documents (Phase III results, FDA decisions) to real-time processing and queues routine documents for Batch API submission. Add correlation IDs for batch result matching. Implement partial failure recovery for batch items. Reference: src/prompts/batch-api-demo.ts demonstrates Batch API usage.

Step 4: Implement Error Classification — Categorize DocumentParser errors as retryable or permanent. Map each error type to a recovery strategy: chunk retry for timeouts, exponential backoff for rate limits, immediate failure for corruption, credential queue for password protection. Preserve partial results from partially successful processing. Reference: src/tools/error-handling.ts provides error classification patterns.

Step 5: Build Multi-Instance Extraction — Implement 3-instance extraction with varied prompts for high-value fields (adverse events). Build the consensus algorithm: 3/3 = confirmed, 2/3 = human review, 1/3 = potential false positive. Calculate cost-accuracy tradeoffs and configure which fields warrant multi-instance extraction vs single-instance. Reference: src/prompts/output-schemas.ts shows output validation patterns.

Step 6: Add Provenance Tracking — Capture source attribution for every extracted value: which section of the document it came from, which extraction instance produced it, and the confidence level. Handle intra-document conflicts by preserving both values with provenance.

Step 7: End-to-End Validation — Process a benchmark set of 100 annotated documents. Measure extraction accuracy per field type, false positive rates for adverse events, batch processing throughput, and cost per document. Verify that the system meets all quality requirements: 95% required field accuracy, 90% optional field accuracy, <1% adverse event false positive rate.
